questions.q: Which statement about lossy compression is correct?
options: The original data can always be perfectly reconstructed
Data is permanently removed and cannot be recovered
It is used for compressing program source code
It never reduces file size as much as lossless
questions.explain: Lossy compression permanently discards data (e.g. JPEG, MP3), so the exact original cannot be recovered, unlike lossless.

questions.q: A black-and-white image has very few runs of identical pixels. What is a likely outcome of applying Run Length Encoding?
options: The file is guaranteed to halve in size
The file may actually become larger
The image quality is reduced
The data becomes unrecoverable
questions.explain: RLE replaces runs with a value plus a count; with few repeats the overhead of storing counts can enlarge the file. RLE is lossless so quality and recoverability are unaffected.

questions.q: In asymmetric encryption, data encrypted with a recipient's public key can be decrypted by which key?
options: The same public key
The sender's private key
The recipient's private key
A shared symmetric key
questions.explain: Public and private keys form a pair; data encrypted with the public key can only be decrypted with the matching private key, solving the key distribution problem.

questions.q: Which property means a hash function always produces the same digest for the same input?
options: Deterministic
One-way
Collision-prone
Reversible
questions.explain: A hash function is deterministic (same input gives same hash), one-way (not reversible) and aims for few collisions.

questions.q: Why are user passwords usually hashed rather than encrypted before storage?
options: Hashing compresses them to save space
Hashing is reversible so they can be recovered
Hashing is one-way so the original cannot be retrieved if stolen
Encryption cannot be applied to text
questions.explain: Hashing is one-way, so a stolen hash does not reveal the password. Encryption is reversible with a key, making it less suitable for password storage.

questions.q: A foreign key is best defined as:
options: A key made of two or more combined attributes
The primary key of another table used to link tables
Any attribute used to speed up searching
An attribute that must always be null
questions.explain: A foreign key is the primary key of another table, used to create a link and enforce referential integrity between the two tables.

questions.q: A table is in 1NF. To reach 2NF, which dependency must be removed?
options: Transitive dependencies
Repeating groups
Partial dependencies
Atomic dependencies
questions.explain: 2NF removes partial dependencies, so every non-key attribute depends on the whole primary key, not just part of a composite key.

questions.q: Which SQL statement returns customer names from a Customers table sorted alphabetically?
options: SELECT Name FROM Customers ORDER BY Name ASC
ORDER Name FROM Customers SELECT ASC
SELECT Customers FROM Name SORT Name
UPDATE Customers SET Name ORDER BY ASC
questions.explain: Correct order is SELECT ... FROM ... ORDER BY. ASC sorts ascending (alphabetically).

questions.q: The 'A' in ACID guarantees that if any step of a transaction fails:
options: The database stays in a partially updated state
All steps are rolled back so no partial change occurs
Other transactions cannot read any data
The change is permanently saved anyway
questions.explain: Atomicity means all-or-nothing: if any step fails the transaction is rolled back so no partial changes are applied.

questions.q: Within a single LAN, which device forwards frames to the correct destination using MAC addresses?
options: Router
Switch
Proxy server
Wireless access point
questions.explain: A switch connects devices within a LAN and uses MAC addresses. A router connects different networks using IP addresses.

questions.q: In the TCP/IP model, which layer adds source and destination IP addresses and routes packets?
options: Application layer
Transport layer
Network/Internet layer
Link layer
questions.explain: The Network/Internet layer adds IP addresses and routes packets. The Link layer adds MAC addresses; the Transport layer handles ports and packet reassembly.

questions.q: Which pair of protocols is correctly described?
options: SMTP retrieves mail; IMAP sends mail
POP3 leaves mail on the server and syncs across devices
SMTP sends mail; IMAP retrieves mail leaving it on the server
FTP encrypts web pages using TLS
questions.explain: SMTP sends mail, IMAP retrieves mail and keeps it on the server (syncing devices), whereas POP3 downloads and removes it.

questions.q: Which factor increases a web page's PageRank the most?
options: Many outgoing links from the page
Incoming links from high-ranking pages
A large number of keywords on the page
Frequent updates to the page content
questions.explain: PageRank treats each incoming link as a vote, and links from high-ranking pages count for more. Outgoing links divide a page's own rank.

questions.q: Why must security-critical checks such as a payment authorisation run server-side rather than only client-side?
options: Client-side code runs faster so is more accurate
Client-side JavaScript can be viewed and bypassed by the user
Server-side code cannot access the database
Client-side code increases server load too much
questions.explain: Client-side code is visible and can be altered or bypassed, so it is insecure; security-critical checks must be enforced server-side where they are hidden and controlled.
